On August 2, 1838, Darwin read a paper by Thomas Malthus on the principle of population. Malthus's ideas stayed in Darwin's mind, and influenced his thinking on evolution. While taking the long, slow boat home from the Galápagos Islands in 1836 with the HMS Beagle, Darwin began to form his ideas on natural selection. After receiving a copy of Wallace's essay, Darwin began writing his own account of the principles of evolution. After enough progress had been made during the voyage, he set his journal down and initially worked on a geological monograph of the Beagle voyage. After Wallace's essay, he shifted his focus to evolution. By June 1858, Darwin had come up with the basic principles of natural selection. He sent a sketch of his theory to his friend and colleague, Charles Lyell, an expert in geology and long-time supporter of natural selection. In July, Lyell informed Darwin that Alfred Russel Wallace, his young student and former helper, had separately come up with natural selection. According to Lyell, Wallace had described the theory in a long letter dated May 1858. Darwin was furious. Lyell immediately arranged a correspondence between Darwin and Wallace to coincide with an abstract by Wallace. Meanwhile, Darwin was enraged over Wallace's published theories. Wallace presented his own paper on natural selection to the Linnaean Society (Part 1) on 1 July 1858. At the same time, immediately following the short abstract of Wallace's paper, Darwin read an essay titled 'On the Tendency of Species to form Varieties; and on the Perpetuation of Varieties and Species by Natural Means of Selection,' which described his own theory of "evolution through natural selection". On 24 January 1859, Darwin read his paper before the Linnaean Society. It described what he called the transmutation of species but called natural selection the "dominant agency". The main biological idea that Darwin described was the application of Malthus’s principle of overproduction to competition among species for existence. <search> On the Origin of Species final book </search>
